metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC #4 that grep-asserts each required string is present in the target files, e.g. `grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always' infra/compose/docker-compose.yml && grep -q 'reviewbot-tickets-pat' infra/terraform/ssm.tf && grep -q 'REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT' infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`.
findings.finding: AC #4 (infra config) lists four concrete file-level changes (docker-compose, Dockerfile, fetch-secrets.sh, ssm.tf) but provides no proving command to verify they are present and correctly formed. All other ACs point to `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py`, which exercises Python logic only — it cannot confirm that `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` appears in `docker-compose.yml`, that the credential-helper stanza is present, or that the SSM leaf and env-var mapping are wired correctly.
findings.location: AC #4: 'Infra config lands in-repo: REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always + a URL-scoped credential helper + persistent tickets worktree in infra/compose/*, the reviewbot-tickets-pat → REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT mapping in fetch-secrets.sh, and the reviewbot-tickets-pat SSM slot in ssm.tf.'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #4: 'Proving command: `grep -q REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always infra/compose/docker-compose.yml && grep -q reviewbot-tickets-pat infra/terraform/ssm.tf && grep -q REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh && grep -q credential.helper infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot && echo ALL_INFRA_OK`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC #5 with a specific observable criterion and proving command, e.g.: 'The code_review sidecar module contains no `max_events` / retention-limit call and the schema sets no cap; proving command: `grep -rn max_events src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py; [ $? -ne 0 ] && echo NO_CAP_OK`' — or explicitly state which code path is retention-free and add a unit-test assertion that the log grows unboundedly under repeated appends.
findings.finding: AC #5 ('No retention cap on the artifact log (deliberate — this is the calibration corpus)') is framed as design intent, not a specific observable outcome, and has no proving command. There is no stated observable state that an executor can verify: 'no retention cap' could mean the schema lacks a `max_events` field, the log-write path never truncates, a config flag is absent, etc. — none of these is named, and none is tested.
findings.location: AC #5: 'No retention cap on the artifact log (deliberate — this is the calibration corpus).'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC #5 as: 'The `code_review` sidecar emit path applies no event-count truncation (no `max_events` guard, no log-rotation call). Proving command: `grep -c max_events src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py | grep -q ^0 && echo NO_RETENTION_CAP_OK`. Unit-tested: appending N>1 verdict events to the same artifact produces N events in the store (added to `test_code_review_artifact.py`).`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Spell out the exact `reads.py` fix: change `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type != "session_log")` (line 373) to `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type not in {"session_log", "code_review"})` so `list --type=code_review` correctly retrieves artifacts rather than silently returning empty.
findings.finding: The `list` command path in `reads.py` uses the expression `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type != 'session_log')` (line 373), which evaluates to `True` when `ticket_type='code_review'`, causing `reduce_all_tickets` to filter out `code_review` tickets *before* the type filter is applied — so `rebar list --type=code_review` would return an empty result even after the new type is added. The plan's instruction to 'generalize the `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type != …)` predicate' in `reads.py` is directionally correct but doesn't spell out the required expression, leaving an implementer who changes only `reducer/_api.py` and misses the `reads.py` call-site with a silent bug.
findings.location: Section A (Visibility) / `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py` line 373
findings.suggested_fix: At `reads.py:373`, replace `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type != "session_log")` with `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type not in {"session_log", "code_review"})`. Add a corresponding unit-test assertion: `list_states(tracker, ticket_type="code_review")` returns the artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git credential helper implementation (script content, install location in Dockerfile, and the precise git config command that URL-scopes it to `https://github.com` only) so the 'token never lands on disk' and 'origin-only scoping' claims are verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'URL-scoped git credential helper' (echoing `x-access-token` / `$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`) will keep the token off disk and off the remote URL, but does not specify the exact git config incantation or where it is installed (Dockerfile layer, entrypoint script, or in-process `git config`). No prior art for this pattern exists in the infra/ codebase — all current remote access uses SSH deploy keys or embedded URLs — and the precise URL-scoping mechanism (`credential.https://github.com.helper` vs global `credential.helper`) determines whether the scoping guarantee actually holds.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'URL-SCOPED (origin only, NOT global) git credential helper'
findings.suggested_fix: In `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, add a minimal helper script (e.g. `/usr/local/bin/rebar-gh-cred-helper.sh` that echoes `username=x-access-token\npassword=$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`) and in the container entrypoint (or a startup script), run: `git config --global credential.https://github.com.helper '/usr/local/bin/rebar-gh-cred-helper.sh'` — URL-scoped to `https://github.com` so Gerrit's SSH remote is unaffected. Document that the helper script itself must be 0700 and that `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` is injected via `.env`, never baked into the image.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or specify) how `rebar.config.tracker_dir()`, `tickets_remote()`, and `push_tickets_branch()` are made to resolve correctly from inside a standalone tickets-branch clone in the container — e.g. by injecting an explicit `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` env var, writing a minimal `rebar.toml` into the clone's parent, or passing an explicit `repo_root` that points to a directory with the correct rebar config.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel runtime topology — a persistent standalone tickets-branch clone inside the reviewbot container — but does not verify that the existing `rebar.config.tracker_dir()`, `tickets_remote()`, and `push_tickets_branch()` machinery (which assumes a git-worktree-linked store derived from a normal repo checkout with a `rebar.toml` or `.rebar/config.conf`) works correctly when called from inside this standalone clone. If `tracker_dir()` or `tickets_remote()` raises `ConfigError` (no rebar config found in the clone), `push_tickets_branch` silently returns early — every artifact is committed locally but never pushed to origin.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the `tickets` branch)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact API change for `reduce_all_tickets`: define the new parameter name and type, whether `exclude_session_logs=True` is a deprecated alias or a breaking change, and how all 5+ existing call sites (graph/_graph.py, _blockers.py, _ready.py, _unblock.py, _hierarchy.py) should be updated.
findings.finding: The plan proposes renaming/broadening `exclude_session_logs` (a boolean parameter) to an artifact-type SET in `reducer/_api.py`, but leaves the migration contract for the 10+ existing call sites that pass `exclude_session_logs=True` unspecified.
findings.location: Section A: 'broaden the single reducer predicate at src/rebar/reducer/_api.py (exclude_session_logs) to cover both artifact types (rename to an artifact-type SET so every downstream guard inherits it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Change `exclude_session_logs: bool` to `exclude_artifact_types: frozenset[str] = frozenset()`, with a `exclude_session_logs: bool = False` compatibility shim that adds `"session_log"` to the set when True. Update all call sites to pass `exclude_artifact_types=frozenset({"session_log", "code_review"})` directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact idempotency lookup mechanism: name the API call (e.g. `search_states` with an exact title match), define the title key format precisely, and state the fallback when the lookup raises (e.g. 'on any lookup error, proceed to create a new artifact and log at WARNING').
findings.finding: The idempotency lookup protocol in Section C ('look up by the change-key title before creating') does not specify which API/field to use for the lookup, nor the fallback behavior when the lookup call itself fails.
findings.location: Section C: 'the artifact is keyed by (change-id, revision) — a re-review of the SAME revision reuses/updates the same artifact (look up by the change-key title before creating) so retries do not duplicate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete API call for the idempotency lookup — either name the `list_tickets` filter + title-match approach (and acknowledge its O(N) cost), or add a dedicated `find_code_review_artifact(change_id, revision)` helper to the plan's scope that searches by a deterministic alias or tag rather than a title string.
findings.finding: The idempotency mechanism — 'look up by the change-key title before creating' — relies on a title-based ticket lookup that has no named API in the rebar library. `resolve_ticket_id` handles id/alias/prefix; `list_tickets` supports `ticket_type` and `parent` filters but not title-equality search. The plan doesn't specify which function performs this lookup, leaving the idempotency implementation unverified at the API-surface level.
findings.location: Section C — 'Idempotency: the artifact is keyed by (change-id, revision) — a re-review of the SAME revision reuses/updates the same artifact (look up by the change-key title before creating)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a deterministic tag (e.g., `tag('code_review_key:<change-id>:<revision)')` on the artifact at creation time, then look up via `list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review', has_tag=...)` — if `has_tag` filtering is supported. Alternatively, use a short deterministic alias derived from `(change_id, revision)` and resolve via `resolve_ticket_id` (alias lookup already exists). The plan should specify which approach is used.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan's Section C/D the explicit mechanism for threading the persistent worktree path to the rebar library write calls in `voter.py` — e.g., set `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` in the container env pointing to the persistent worktree's `.tickets-tracker`, or pass an explicit `repo_root=<persistent_worktree_path>` parameter to each `rebar.create_ticket` / `rebar.link` / `sidecar.emit` call.
findings.finding: The plan's infra section specifies a 'PERSISTENT tickets worktree' in the container for the rebar library write path (create artifact, emit sidecar, link), but does not specify what `repo_root` value is passed to `rebar.create_ticket(...)`, `rebar.link(...)`, and `code_review.sidecar.emit(...)` in `voter.py`. The existing per-review `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` is ephemeral and unsuitable. Without a specified `repo_root` pointing to the persistent worktree, the rebar library calls will attempt to discover the tracker via the default `config.tracker_dir()` path, which is undefined inside the reviewbot container for the artifact write path.
findings.location: Section C — emit/link calls in `voter.py`; Section D — 'on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree once'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot/tickets-tracker` in the container environment (alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always`), and initialize the persistent worktree at that path on container startup. The rebar library's `config.tracker_dir()` will then resolve it via the env var override without needing an explicit `repo_root` parameter on each call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git credential helper mechanism in Section D: name the config key (e.g., `git config --local credential.https://github.com.helper '/path/to/echo-helper.sh'`), provide the helper script content or a reference implementation, and add an AC that the helper is verified NOT to write to disk (e.g., `git credential fill` produces output but `git config --list` shows no `credential.helper=store`).
findings.finding: The credential helper implementation is underspecified on the boundary-crossing push path: the plan states the intent ('URL-scoped origin only, NOT global, echoes username/password, token never lands on disk') but does not specify the concrete git mechanism (e.g., `git config --local credential.https://github.com.helper /path/to/helper-script` vs `GIT_ASKPASS` vs `credential.helper store`). An implementer choosing the wrong wiring — such as `git credential store` or inlining the token in a `git remote set-url` — would silently persist the PAT to disk or the remote URL, defeating the stated intent. The access posture is declared ('never on disk or in a remote URL') but the enforcement mechanism is not specified precisely enough to prevent the defect.
findings.location: Section D (Infra): 'configure a URL-SCOPED (origin only, NOT global) git credential helper that echoes `username=x-access-token` / `password=$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'Implement the credential helper as a small shell script (e.g., `/usr/local/bin/rebar-git-cred-helper.sh`) installed in the Dockerfile that reads `$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` from env and writes `username=x-access-token\npassword=$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` to stdout. Wire it with `git config --local credential.https://github.com.helper /usr/local/bin/rebar-git-cred-helper.sh` in the worktree init step (NOT `--global`). Explicitly exclude `git credential store`, `git credential cache`, and token-in-URL patterns. Add an AC smoke-check: after init, `git config --local credential.helper` returns the script path and `git config --local --get-all credential.helper` contains no `store` or `cache` entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit idempotency test case to `tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py`: call the emit path twice with identical `(change-id, revision)` against a fake store and assert exactly one artifact is created and no duplicate `relates_to` links are emitted.
findings.finding: The idempotency behavior — 'a re-review of the SAME revision reuses/updates the same artifact' — is stated in AC#3 but the explicit test scenarios listed only cover two cases: 'resolvable ref → link; unresolved ref → logged, not raised'. A retry/duplicate-emit scenario (second call with identical change-id/revision does NOT create a second artifact) is not called out as a named test case, leaving the idempotency contract untested in the plan's test spec.
findings.location: AC line 3 — 'Unit-tested with a fake store (resolvable ref → link; unresolved ref → logged, not raised)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file, add a scenario: `store.create_ticket` mock is called once on a second identical emit call (the lookup finds the existing artifact and skips creation); assert `store.link` is not called a second time for already-linked refs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly list the `_hierarchy.py` blocking-link refusal check (lines ~175-183) as a concrete site to extend with `code_review`, and specify whether it should be a `ticket_type in {"session_log", "code_review"}` set-check to avoid future divergence.
findings.finding: The `_TYPE_TIER` dict in `graph/_hierarchy.py` and the per-type name-check for blocking-link refusal both hardcode `session_log` by name, but the plan does not list `graph/_hierarchy.py`'s `_TYPE_TIER` dict or the blocking-link refusal check (lines 175/181) as sites requiring `code_review` addition — only `exclude_session_logs` predicate broadening is mentioned.
findings.location: Section A scope list for graph/*.py; graph/_hierarchy.py lines 25, 175, 181
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section A: 'In graph/_hierarchy.py, extend both the `source_state.get("ticket_type") == "session_log"` and `target_state.get("ticket_type") == "session_log"` blocking-link refusal guards to `in {"session_log", "code_review"}`, and add `"code_review": 0` to `_TYPE_TIER`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the actual type-to-Jira-issuetype mapping symbol(s) in `outbound_fields.py` and `reconcile_check.py` (search for `ticket_type`, `issuetype`, or `jira_type` mappings) and replace the non-existent `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` reference in the plan with the real symbol name(s) and the correct action.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'leave BOTH `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` maps (`outbound_fields.py`, `reconcile_check.py`) untouched so any leak surfaces' — but a search of both files finds zero occurrences of `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` (or any `LOCAL_TO_JIRA` variant). The named symbol does not exist in the named files.
findings.location: Section A, Jira exclusion bullet: '`_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` maps (`outbound_fields.py`, `reconcile_check.py`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section C step 2 that `sidecar.build_payload` must be extended to accept and embed `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id` (not just called as-is), and add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` to the Scope/files list for the emit+keys bullet.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` 'already writes a `REVIEW_RESULT` event' and frames Section C step 2 as reusing `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` for the emit. However, `sidecar.emit` currently has no `change_fingerprint` or per-finding `norm_id` in its `build_payload` — the plan requires AUGMENTING this payload (adding those fields), but it describes the call as a reuse of an existing emit path. The existing `build_payload` signature takes only `verdict` and `target_ticket`, with no parameters for `change_fingerprint` or `norm_id`.
findings.location: Section C, step 2: 'reuse the existing `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)` → `code_review.sidecar.emit`, which already writes a `REVIEW_RESULT` event, augmented with `change_fingerprint` and per-finding `norm_id`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` to the Scope/files 'Emit + keys' line and update step 2 to say: 'extend `code_review.sidecar.build_payload` to accept `change_fingerprint` and embed per-finding `norm_id`, then call via `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body entry (e.g., in Section D or a new Section E) that explicitly describes what 'no retention cap' means concretely (e.g., no log-rotation config on the tickets-branch worktree, no TTL on the REVIEW_RESULT events), and add a corresponding verification step (config inspection or a unit assertion) to AC5.
findings.finding: AC5 ('No retention cap on the artifact log') is an orphan criterion: no section of the plan body describes any work — no code change, config entry, or design decision — that would implement or verify a retention policy (or its deliberate absence). There is also no proving command for AC5, unlike ACs 1–4.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'No retention cap on the artifact log (deliberate — this is the calibration corpus).'
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D, add a bullet: 'Retention: no log-rotation or TTL policy is applied to the tickets branch or REVIEW_RESULT event log — the artifact accumulates indefinitely as the calibration corpus. Verify by confirming no cron/logrotate entry for the worktree and no TTL param in the sidecar emit call.' Then give AC5 a proving command (e.g., a grep assertion in CI or a unit test checking the emit call has no ttl= kwarg).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete observable proof to AC#5: either a test asserting no TTL/retention field is set on `code_review` artifact records, a schema check, or a prose description of which config location to inspect and what value confirms no cap — so the criterion is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC#5 ('No retention cap on the artifact log') asserts a deliberate policy but provides no observable proof or proving command — there is no test, schema constraint check, or store-configuration assertion that verifies the absence of a cap.
findings.location: AC line 5 — 'No retention cap on the artifact log (deliberate — this is the calibration corpus).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a startup health-check that fails loudly (logged ERROR + container readiness probe failure) if the tickets worktree clone fails, and document a shallow-clone or periodic-GC strategy (even if deferred) for long-term branch growth.
findings.finding: No retirement or compaction path is specified for the `code_review` artifacts accumulating on the `tickets` branch, and the worktree startup-failure mode (e.g., clone fails due to bad PAT or network) is not specified — it is unclear whether the reviewbot container fails to start, starts in degraded mode, or retries.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) / AC #5 ('No retention cap on the artifact log (deliberate)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the minimum-privilege posture of the REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT in Section D: state explicitly that the PAT is scoped to the single repository `navapbc/rebar` only (not org-wide), that `contents:write` is the least GitHub fine-grained permission that permits `git push`, and acknowledge that branch-level restriction to `tickets` is unavailable in GitHub's PAT model (so this scope is accepted as the practical minimum).
findings.finding: The REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT permission scope is named ('contents:write') but its minimum-privilege posture is undeclared: the plan does not state whether this is the least-permissive GitHub scope available for this operation, whether the PAT is restricted to the single `navapbc/rebar` repository, or acknowledge that GitHub fine-grained PATs cannot restrict writes to a specific branch (so the PAT permits push to any branch of the repo, not just `tickets`). Per the rubric, the finding is an undeclared access posture, not a missing control.
findings.location: Section D (Infra): 'the operator populates with the RebarBotNava contents:write PAT'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the SSM slot description in Section D: '(fine-grained PAT scoped to the single repo `navapbc/rebar`, `contents:write` only — the least permission GitHub offers for `git push`; branch-level restriction to `tickets` is not available in GitHub's fine-grained PAT model and this is accepted)'. Add a corresponding AC bullet: 'The PAT is a fine-grained token restricted to `navapbc/rebar` only (not org-wide); the runbook documents this constraint.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section C whether 'ALL commits' means only the tip commit message (already fetched) or requires iterating all commits in the change; if the latter, name the Gerrit API endpoint (e.g. `GET /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/commit` for each commit in the mergelist) and add it to the voter's emit path.
findings.finding: The plan states trailer resolution calls `verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` 'over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)' — but in voter.py the only commit message fetched is from `gc.get_commit(change_id, revision)`, which returns a single commit. No mechanism for fetching all commit messages across a multi-commit change is named or exists in the voter. The 'ALL commits' language implies multi-commit traversal, yet no Gerrit API call that enumerates all commits in a change is referenced.
findings.location: Section C ('TRAILER RESOLUTION') / voter.py ~line 364-368
findings.suggested_fix: If single-commit (tip) is the intended scope, change 'over ALL commits in the change' to 'from the tip commit message (the single commit fetched by gc.get_commit)' to avoid the ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (and implement) how 'ALL commits in the change' are fetched: either (a) restrict to the HEAD commit message already in scope (simplest, document the limitation), or (b) add a `GerritClient` method to list all revision commit messages and specify the Gerrit endpoint used (`GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/` or walking parents).
findings.finding: The plan specifies `verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` 'over ALL commits in the change,' but the voter's `review_and_vote` only fetches ONE commit message — the change's HEAD revision via `gc.get_commit(change_id, revision)` (voter.py ~366). Iterating all commits in a Gerrit change requires additional REST calls (e.g., `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/` listing or walking the mergelist) that are not currently in the `GerritClient` API surface and are not specified in the plan.
findings.location: Section C — 'TRAILER RESOLUTION: verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over ALL commits in the change'
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest correct fix is to restrict trailer resolution to the single commit message already fetched (`commit_message` from `gc.get_commit`) and document that multi-commit changes are resolved against the HEAD commit only. If full multi-commit coverage is needed, add `GerritClient.list_revision_commit_messages(change_id)` using `GET /a/changes/{id}?o=ALL_REVISIONS&o=CURRENT_COMMIT` and spec it explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how all commit messages for a multi-commit Gerrit change are retrieved in Section C (e.g., 'call `gerrit_client.list_commits(change_id, revision)` — already available in the reviewbot context — then union `extract_ticket_refs` over each message'), so the executor does not need to investigate or guess.
findings.finding: The plan says trailer resolution iterates 'ALL commits in the change' and 'parse each commit's message,' but `voter.py`'s in-scope variable is a single `commit_message` for the current review. The plan does not specify how to retrieve the full list of commit messages for a multi-commit Gerrit change (e.g., an additional Gerrit REST API call, a pre-existing helper, or an already-aggregated field). An executor cannot implement this without guessing or stopping to investigate.
findings.location: Section C — Trailer resolution paragraph: 'verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence: 'Retrieve all commit messages via `gerrit_client.list_commits(change_id, revision)` (already available in the reviewbot context); pass each message to `extract_ticket_refs` and union the resulting refs. If the client wrapper does not yet expose this call, use the single `commit_message` already in scope and document the single-commit limitation in a TODO.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback/disable path for `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always`: either document a concrete back-out step (e.g., 'set REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off and redeploy to revert to ephemeral-only behavior') or add a runtime guard (e.g., if PAT env var is absent/empty, log a warning and skip push rather than erroring) so the container degrades gracefully without a full redeploy.
findings.finding: Setting `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` in the reviewbot container is a behavioral change to existing container operation, but the plan provides no rollback path: if the credential helper is misconfigured or the PAT is invalid, the reviewbot container would fail every push with no described recovery procedure.
findings.location: Section D — 'infra/compose/{docker-compose.yml,Dockerfile.reviewbot} — set REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always; on startup materialize a PERSISTENT tickets worktree'
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D, add: 'Rollback: if the PAT is invalid or push fails persistently, set REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off (or leave REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT unset) and redeploy — the container falls back to ephemeral-only behavior. The push step already catches and logs failures non-fatally, so votes are unaffected.' This makes the back-out path explicit and actionable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note for the `exclude_session_logs` → artifact-type SET rename: either (a) describe an expand-contract sequence (add the SET alongside the old name, migrate callers, then remove the old name in a follow-up), or (b) document a concrete rollback step (e.g., 'revert the _api.py commit and redeploy') and confirm the rename is exhaustively audited via a grep/ripgrep check in CI.
findings.finding: The rename of `exclude_session_logs` (a symbol consumed by multiple downstream guard modules) has no rollback path or expand-contract sequence described. If the rename breaks an unlisted caller or is deployed with a bug, there is no described mechanism to revert the store/guard behavior to its pre-change state.
findings.location: Section A — 'broaden the single reducer predicate at src/rebar/reducer/_api.py (exclude_session_logs) to cover both artifact types (rename to an artifact-type SET so every downstream guard inherits it)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the required deploy order (store-side type changes first, then reviewbot container) and add it as an explicit step in the deployment runbook or infra/compose README.
findings.finding: The deploy ordering between the store's type-system changes (schema, composer, reducer, graph guards) and the updated reviewbot container is unspecified, leaving a window where the container attempts to create `code_review` tickets before the store accepts that type.
findings.location: Section A (type guard sites) / Section C / Section D

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the 'bulk artifact type' pattern (gate-exempt, graph-exempt, Jira-excluded, lifecycle-exempt, hidden-from-list) and the canonical checklist of guard sites any future artifact type must replicate.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel 'bulk artifact' type pattern (gate-exempt, graph-exempt, Jira-excluded, lifecycle-exempt, hidden from list) that must be replicated at ~15+ guard sites and will need to be replicated again for any future artifact type — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc captures this pattern for future contributors.
findings.location: Section A ('mirror session_log at EVERY guard site') and Scope/files list
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/004-bulk-artifact-types.md`) or a CLAUDE.md section titled 'Artifact-only ticket types' that lists: (1) the intent/invariants, (2) the canonical guard-site checklist (the ~15 files in Section A), and (3) the `exclude_session_logs` → artifact-type-SET rename as the extensibility mechanism. Reference it from the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one instrumentation point (e.g. a structured log counter, a Prometheus/StatsD metric, or a health-check in the reviewbot) that tracks artifact-write success/failure rates so operators can alert on a degraded corpus pipeline.
findings.finding: Observability for the artifact-write pipeline (create ticket, emit sidecar, push to tickets branch) is log-only; there is no metric, counter, or alert specified for failed artifact creation or push failures, so operators cannot detect a degraded or silently-dropping corpus without grepping logs.
findings.location: Section C (log format spec) and Section D (infra)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the lookup-then-create idempotency pattern with an atomic upsert or a unique-key enforcement at the store layer (e.g., a store-level constraint that rejects duplicate ticket titles within the `code_review` type, returning the existing artifact ID on conflict).
findings.finding: The idempotency mechanism ('look up by the change-key title before creating') is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) pattern: two concurrent callers (e.g., a manual retrigger racing the normal background worker, or a future multi-worker scenario) can both observe 'no artifact exists' and both proceed to create one, duplicating artifacts.
findings.location: Section C — 'Idempotency: the artifact is keyed by (change-id, revision) — a re-review of the SAME revision reuses/updates the same artifact (look up by the change-key title before creating)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the proposed `exclude_session_logs` bool→SET rename with a simpler two-condition check (`r.get('ticket_type') not in {"session_log", "code_review"}`) at reducer/_api.py:244, preserving the existing bool API and avoiding a 6-site call-site update.
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'rename [exclude_session_logs] to an artifact-type SET so every downstream guard inherits it' — but with only 2 artifact types needing exclusion (session_log, code_review), this API-breaking refactor is premature abstraction: the 6 existing call sites all pass `exclude_session_logs=True` (a bool) and would all need updating, yet the identical outcome can be achieved with a single additional `or r.get('ticket_type') == 'code_review'` condition at reducer/_api.py:244 with zero call-site changes.
findings.location: Section A, 'Store/graph exclusion'; src/rebar/reducer/_api.py:241-244
findings.suggested_fix: At reducer/_api.py:241-244, change the condition to `if exclude_session_logs: results = [r for r in results if r.get('ticket_type') not in {"session_log", "code_review"}]` — no parameter-type change, no call-site updates needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a retry policy (e.g. up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff, then log at ERROR) for the git push to the `tickets` branch so transient failures do not silently drop corpus entries.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that push failures to the `tickets` branch are caught and logged but does not specify any retry or backoff for transient git-push failures, meaning network blips or temporary ref conflicts will silently drop corpus entries with no recovery path.
findings.location: Section D ('Infra') and Section C ('All emit/link/push failures are caught and logged; they NEVER fail the review vote')
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D (or Section C's failure-handling clause), add: 'The push is retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s); on final failure, log at ERROR level with the artifact id and change coordinates so the corpus gap is detectable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an env-var toggle (e.g. `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ARTIFACT_EMIT=true/false`, defaulting to `false` until validated in staging) so the artifact-emit behavior can be enabled/disabled without redeploying a different image.
findings.finding: No feature flag or staged rollout gate exists for the artifact-emit behavior: once the new container is deployed, 100% of gerrit reviews will attempt to create `code_review` artifacts and push to the `tickets` branch, with no way to enable for a subset of changes first.
findings.location: Section C (Reviewbot emit) / Section D (Infra) / AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: In voter.py, guard the entire artifact-create block with `if os.getenv('REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ARTIFACT_EMIT', 'false').lower() == 'true':`. Default to `false` in docker-compose.yml and flip to `true` after a canary period.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether a 'quality-check' gate exists (search across `_engine_support/`, gate YAML files, and the bash dispatcher), correct the plan's gate-exemption list to name only real gates, and add any real quality-gate exemption site to Section A's concrete sites list.
findings.finding: The plan asserts gate-exemption for 'check-ac / clarity / quality' in `gates.py`, but `gates.py` contains no 'quality' gate function — only `check_ac_compute` and `clarity_check_compute` carry the `session_log` exemption. A 'quality-check' gate does not exist in that file.
findings.location: Section A, Gate exemption bullet: '`_engine_support/gates.py` (check-ac / clarity / quality → gate-exempt pass)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py` (and the `recent_session_logs_state` type filter in `reads.py`) to the scope/file list and specify whether code_review artifacts need a parallel listing command or whether the existing filter is intentionally left session_log-only.
findings.finding: src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py is an unenumerated consumer: it contains a session_log-specific subcommand (_cmd_session_logs, registered as 'session-logs' in _COMMANDS) and imports/calls recent_session_logs_state, which itself hardcodes `ticket_type == 'session_log'`. The plan lists reads.py but not reads_cli.py — so the 'session-logs' CLI dispatch and the recent_session_logs_state type filter are both un-addressed consumers of the session_log type string.
findings.location: Section A ('Visibility: _engine_support/reads.py') / Scope file list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` to the scope file list and specify whether a `'code_reviews'` / `'code_reviews.llm'` OUTPUT_SCHEMAS entry is needed (or explicitly state it is not required).
findings.finding: src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py is an unenumerated consumer: it has a hardcoded 'session_logs' key in OUTPUT_SCHEMAS (line 252/256) that maps the session-logs subcommand output to TICKET_STATE. If a code_review listing command is added (reads_cli.py), a corresponding OUTPUT_SCHEMAS entry would be needed; even if not, the file contains session_log-specific schema registration that may need updating.
findings.location: Section A scope / 'Scope / files' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Section B norm_id description to clarify it is a single-argument function `norm_id(finding: dict)` where `criteria` is read from `finding['criteria']` — the parenthetical `(finding, criteria)` is misleading.
findings.finding: The plan describes norm_id as taking 'a finding dict (`finding`, `criteria`)' — implying two arguments. The actual function signature is `norm_id(finding: dict[str, Any]) -> str` with criteria extracted internally via `finding.get('criteria')`. This is not a hallucination of the function, but the parenthetical description is wrong in a way that could confuse the implementer who augments the sidecar payload to ensure findings have the right shape.
findings.location: Section B ('norm_id IS reused verbatim: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:210 norm_id(finding) takes a finding dict (`finding`, `criteria`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: '`norm_id(finding)` takes a single finding dict that must carry `finding` and `criteria` keys — code-review findings share that shape, so per-finding identity reuses it with NO new algorithm.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gate_dispatch.py` to the Scope/files list and explicitly specify that `produce_code_review_verdict` must be IMPLEMENTED there (not merely called as if already existing), or document the exact existing call path in `shim.py` that the emit step should use instead.
findings.finding: `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` is referenced in Section C step 2 as an existing callable to reuse, but the function does not exist in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py`.
findings.location: Section C, step 2: 'reuse the existing gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(..., target_ticket=<artifact id>)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reuse the existing gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(...)' with 'call the existing `shim.produce_code_review_verdict` / `adapter.code_review_decision` result already held in `decision` at this point in voter.py — no gate_dispatch call is needed; the verdict is already in scope'. Alternatively, if a new gate_dispatch wrapper is truly needed, add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` to the Scope/files and define the function's signature.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
